journal-title-group.journal-title: International journal on physical medication & rehabilitation

title-group.article-title: Long Duration Continuous Ultrasound with Diclofenac Sodium Enhances Recovery from Sprains and Strains

article-meta.aff: 1Medical Pain Consultants, Cayuga Medical Center, USA
3Consultants in Sports Medicine, Columbia, SC, USA

fn.p: All authors contributed to the conception and design of the study. All the authors analyzed the data. Ralph Ortiz wrote the first draft of the manuscript, and all authors approved the final manuscript.

author-notes.corresp: *Corresponding author: Rod Walters DA, ATC, Consultants in Sports Medicine, Columbia, SC, 112 Twelve Mile Dr, Lexington, SC 29072-3964, USA

license.license-p: This work is published and licensed by Example Press Limited. The full terms of this license are available at https://skeenapublishers.com/terms-conditions and incorporate the Creative Commons Attribution – Non Commercial (unported, v3.0) License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/). By accessing the work you hereby accept the Terms. Non-commercial uses of the work are permitted without any further permission from Emample Press, provided the work is properly attributed.

sec.title: Purpose:
sec.p: Musculoskeletal sprains and strains cause inflammation and scar tissue formation with impaired healing and tissue regeneration due to limited blood supply. Diclofenac Sodium, a Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug, has analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antipyretic properties. Low-intensity continuous ultrasound (LICUS) therapy provides mechanical and thermal stimuli to enhance blood flow and oxygenation, promoting tissue healing and facilitating transdermal drug delivery. This study determines the synergistic effects of low-intensity continuous ultrasound therapy and a 2.5% diclofenac sodium ultrasound gel patch in treating ankle and elbow strains and sprains.

sec.title: Methods:
sec.p: The cohort of participants (n=8) with ankle or elbow strain or sprain with no prior treatment was selected to determine baseline range of motion (ROM, 0 – 100%), numerical rating scale (NRS, 0–10) pain score, and global rating of change score (GroC, −7 to +7). All participants were treated for 14 days with LICUS + 2.5% diclofenac treatment daily for 4 hours.

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: The participants’ ROM increased from 67.5 ±12.25 % to 95 ±5.39% (28% increase, p<0.0001), and an NRS score of 7.25 ± 0.71 was significantly reduced by 2.75 ± 0.71. Treatment improved the participants’ global rating of change score by 6.34 ± 0.92.

sec.title: Conclusion:
sec.p: The pilot study demonstrates the synergistic effects of LICUS with diclofenac in treating ankle and elbow strains and sprains. It reduces pain and improves participants’ overall quality of life, improving ROM, GRoC, and overall quality of life without adverse effects.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Overextension or tearing of ligaments (sprains) and muscles/tendons (strains) are the most prevalent sports injuries [1]. Military training and athletes in competitive sports face consistent performance demands, and the overuse of musculoskeletal tissues increases the occurrence of sprains and strains [2,3]. In the United States alone, approximately 1.5 million sprain and strain injuries are reported annually, with ankle sprains constituting up to 40% of sports-related injuries [4]. Mechanical stress can lead to localized damage in ligaments and tendons, triggering inflammation and scar tissue formation [5]. Efficient tissue healing and regeneration are crucial to ensure the return to play (RTP). Due to the limited blood supply and consistent physical demand, the healing of these injuries is severely impaired. Rushing the healing process can result in fatigue, chronic inflammation, worsening of the injury, and impaired tissue regeneration, with a higher likelihood of repeated injuries and significant underperformance [2,6,7].

Athletes’ timely RTP and optimal performance are vital for their careers [8,9] and the professional sports teams they support. There is a significant financial impact through ticket sales, viewership, and sponsorship deals for athletes to be available and ready to play [10]. The National Athletic Trainers’ Association (NATA) has recommended timely diagnoses and treatment of sprain and strain injuries with RICE (Rest, Ice, Compression, and Elevation), followed by administration of Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) orally or topically to reduce pain and swelling along with an extensive rehabilitation program [11]. RICE is effective in the acute phase of injury with the short-term control of the inflammatory response, limiting swelling and reducing hypoxic damage, but studies have shown limited long-term benefits of RICE treatment [12]. NSAIDs have been shown to be effective in reducing pain in the acute stage of healing [13]. Still, studies have shown sprains treated with NSAIDs have greater instability, less range of motion (ROM), and more swelling, potentially due to impaired healing in the acute stage. The long-term use of oral NSAIDs has been shown to have adverse systemic effects, which are significantly reduced with the application of topical targeted administration of NSAIDs [14–17].

Diclofenac Sodium, a common NSAID, has analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antipyretic properties [18]. It inhibits cyclooxygenase-1 and 2, thereby modulating the production of prostaglandin and various pro-inflammatory cytokines, including tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNFα), interleukin-6 (IL-6), and interleukin-8 (IL-8) [19]. The oral administration of diclofenac has been associated with adverse vascular and gastrointestinal effects [20]. While topical application of diclofenac has demonstrated effectiveness in conditions such as osteoarthritis, rheumatic arthritis, and soft tissue injuries like sprains, ligament tears, and contusions, the efficacy of topical diclofenac is limited due to its restricted penetration through the skin tissue [16,21].

Ultrasound (US) is a form of high-frequency mechanical wave providing mechanical and thermal stimulus [22–24]. Tendons and ligaments are known to respond to mechanical and thermal stimuli, significantly increasing the blood flow and oxygenation [25,26]. Studies have shown that the US has proliferative and anti-inflammatory effects that expedite tissue healing and regeneration [23,27,28]. Furthermore, the mechanical and thermal effects enhance transdermal porosity, making it an effective tool for targeted transdermal drug delivery [29–31]. Sustained acoustic medicine or long duration multi-hour ultrasound has been reported to be efficacious in providing essential mechanical and thermal stimuli, alleviating musculoskeletal pain [32–34].

Sustained acoustic medicine (SAM) is a Federal Drug Administration (FDA) approved medical device that delivers high-frequency long-duration ultrasound at 1.3W and 0.132 W/cm2, with 5,000 joules per hour energy for a 4-hour treatment session [24,35,36]. Meta-analyses conducted by Winkler et al. report SAM’s clinical efficacy in treating musculoskeletal disorders, including myofascial pain, rotator cuff tendinopathy, knee osteoarthritis (OA), and upper shoulder and neck discomfort [36]. Additionally, studies by Madzia et al. indicate that combining SAM therapy with 1% topical diclofenac enhances pain relief and improves quality of life, measured by the Western Ontario McMaster Outcome (WOMAC) scale, after 7-day treatment [37]. Jarit et al. observed significant improvements in pain levels and overall function in musculoskeletal injury healing, which was previously reported as unresponsive to conventional physical therapy after a 4-week SAM with a 2.5% diclofenac treatment regimen [38].

Recent strides in biomedical technology have facilitated the conjunction of medical devices with biological interventions, allowing the development of innovative, targeted, and more effective therapies. These advancements aim to expedite and regulate the healing process, minimizing the downtime required for athletes to return to play. This study aims to determine the efficacy of SAM with a 2.5% diclofenac ultrasound gel patch effectiveness in treating ankle and elbow strains and sprains.

sec.title: Study Design
sec.p: Participants in the study followed the normal standard of care with the addition of SAM with 2.5% diclofenac gel daily treatment. An IRB-approved data collection form and study materials were utilized (Advarra IRB), and the study was registered in the United States (NCT05254470) for completion over the 2021–2022 hockey season. Connecticut Whale professional women’s ice hockey players with ankle or elbow strain or sprain diagnosed by a board-certified team physician were selected for the study. Before initiating any treatment, baseline range of motion (ROM, 0 – 100%), numerical rating scale (NRS, 0–10) pain score, and global rating of change score (GroC, −7 to +7) were recorded. All patients were treated for 14 days with SAM administered by the team sports medicine staff daily. All the outcomes were recorded before the treatment and after 14 days of the treatment by a board-certified team physician (Figure 1).

sec.title: Procedure
sec.p: Eight professional (n=8) ice hockey players with grade II injuries (considered play-stopping or significantly impaired by the player and healthcare team) were selected for the study. The SAM device (model sam-12, Zetr OZ Systems LLC., Trumbull, CT) with a pair of small rechargeable wireless transducers clipped onto an adhesive ultrasound patch pre-loaded with 2.5% w/v diclofenac coupling patch (Compound Solutions, Monroe, CT) was directly applied to the skin above the treatment area (ankle or elbow). The addition of diclofenac sodium did not significantly change ultrasound gel acoustic impedance and speed of ultrasound through the gel, as previously reported by Hendren et al. [39] The site of injury was treated for 4 hours daily for 14 days. The patients were trained to use the device by qualified physicians and/or physician assistants and to keep a daily diary of changes in pain, changes in mobility, and any observed rashes or discoloration of the skin.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: The Pearson normality test and two-tailed T-test were conducted using Prism GraphPad (Version 10.2.2).Two-tailed T-tests were conducted, and the mean with standard deviation was recorded. The statistical differences of more than p<0.05 were considered significant differences. Estimation analyses were conducted to establish the magnitude of pre- and post-treatment differences for each patient.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Quality of life: The patients’ diaries recorded changes in quality of life over the duration of treatment. All the patients reported improved mobility, pain reduction, and overall quality of life improvement.

Range of Motion (ROM): Ankle and elbow strains and sprain were treated with SAM with 2.5% diclofenac ultrasound gel for 4 hours for 14 days. The subjects’ ROM before treatment was restricted to 67.5 ±12.25 %. After 14 days of the treatment regime, the ROM increased to 95 ±5.39%, demonstrating a significant increase of 28% (p<0.0001. Figure. 2A, B). The estimation plot analysis illustrates the magnitude of improvement in the ROM of each patient (Figure. 2B).

Pain numeric rating scale (NRS): The treatment significantly reduced the pain over 14 days of treatment as quantified through NRS. The patients reported a mean NRS score of 7.25 ± 0.71 before the 14-day treatment. A significant reduction in the NRS of 2.75 ± 0.71 was recorded (p<0.0001. Figure 3A), with a mean decrease of 4.5 ± 1.31(Figure 3B). The estimation plot illustrated the magnitude of pain reduction for each subject before and after the 14 days of treatment (Figure 3B).
Global Rating of Change Score (GRoC): GRoC evaluates the overall improvement of patients’ condition. Treatment improved the patient’s GRoC by 6.34 ± 0.92 after 14 days of treatment (Figure 4).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Efficient and expedited healing is vital for an athlete’s effective RTP. Musculoskeletal injuries, particularly ankle and elbow sprains, are prevalent among athletes [1]. The healing process of tendons and ligaments is often slow, which leads to impaired healing, recurrent injuries, and underperformance [8,9,40]. To ensure regulated and accelerated recovery, regulating inflammation during the acute phase prevents chronic inflammation and expedites tissue regeneration [5,41].

SAM and NSAIDs have been shown to reduce inflammation and accelerate tissue regeneration [13,20,42,43]. The study determines the co-adjuvant effects of combining long-duration ultrasound and diclofenac by applying SAM with 2.5% diclofenac ultrasound gel to enhance the transdermal targeted delivery of diclofenac and provide essential mechanical and thermal stimuli to accelerate tissue healing and alleviate pain. The study reports a significant reduction in NRS pain score and improved ROM and GRoC after 14 days of treatment. Athletes reported enhanced mobility, reduced pain, and improved performance in daily activities, with no adverse effects observed during treatment. Tendons and ligaments are fibrous tissues with limited blood supply, thus requiring a prolonged healing time of up to 12 months. This has significant psychological and physical effects on the athletes and an economic impact on the team. Reducing pain and improving mobility and quality of life can go a long way in motivating athletes and improving their mental health.

The application of SAM with diclofenac can significantly decrease the duration of healing and acute and chronic inflammation and enhance the remodeling process. SAM is a home-use wearable medical device approved by the FDA [32,44] Thus, it can be used without interfering with daily physical activities and keeping physically active and motivated.

Targeted delivery of diclofenac reduces the systemic adverse effects of NSAIDs and, in conjunction with SAM, improves diclofenac’s bio-availability at the injury site, regulating acute inflammation and relieving pain. SAM provides the essential stimulus to enhance the skin porosity and targeted drug delivery through a mechanism known as sonophoresis, the ability of ultrasound to create localized cavitation, increasing skin porosity and enhancing drug delivery deep into the skin tissue. The diathermic effects of SAM allow increased blood circulation, oxygenation, reduced inflammation, and nutrient exchange [39,45–47]. The pilot study demonstrates the potential long-term efficacy of SAM with diclofenac ultrasound gel treatment. However, our pilot study did not directly compare SAM with diclofenac treatment to standard care (RICE and physical therapy), SAM, or topical diclofenac treatment alone. Conventional treatments have been reported to have limited short-term benefits and little or no long-term effects [12]. The efficacy of topical diclofenac application has been well established but limited due to transdermal delivery [21]. SAM treatment effectively mitigates pain, and recent studies have shown transdermal diclofenac delivery [37,48].The study provides a 3b level of evidence clinical study. It provides clinical evidence of SAM as an effective medical device to deliver LICUS for targeted transdermal drug delivery and diclofenac to treat ankle and elbow strains in athletes, improving ROM, reducing pain, and improving overall quality of life after 14 days of treatment. The current study provides encouraging data but is limited by its sample size and in segregating between sprain and strain categorization. Future research will include extensive radiographic analysis to define the degree of sprain and strain as initial inclusion and excuslion criteria, and the tissue healing over time with a larger sample size over an extended period to evaluate SAM’s efficacy with diclofenac ultrasound gel treatment.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The pilot study demonstrates the co-adjuvant effects of SAM with diclofenac in treating ankle and elbow strains and sprains. It reduces pain and improves athletes’ overall quality of life, improving ROM, GroC, and overall quality of life without adverse effects. The data would help to design future studies establishing the clinical efficacy of SAM with diclofenac ultrasound gel in treating musculoskeletal pain, expediting healing, and enhancing tissue remodeling. The study provides encouraging data to test the SAM diclofenac regime as a valuable tool in musculoskeletal pain management, tissue healing, and regeneration.

ack.title: Funding
ack.p: The authors declare that no funds, grants, or other support were received in conducting this study and preparing this manuscript.

fn.p: The authors have no relevant financial or non-financial interests to disclose.

All human participants signed the consent-to-participate form, and the institutional research board approved the study. The study was performed following the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: Data will be available upon request.

caption.p: The study design illustrates the basic design of the study and its outcomes.
Range of Motion: A) The change of ROM in patients before and after 14-day SAM treatment, B) Estimation analysis illustrating the magnitude of change in ROM for each patient and mean change in ROM.

Pain numeric rating scale (NRS): A) The change of NRS in patients before and after the 14-day SAM treatment, B) Estimation analysis illustrating the magnitude of change in pain for each patient and mean change in NRS.
Global Rating of Change Score (GroC) demonstrating the change in functionality and overall quality of life after 14 days of SAM treatment.
